behaviors.description: A cell containing =SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum of the values in cells A1 through A10.
behaviors.justification: Testing this behavior requires the formula parser, range resolver (A1:A10), cell reference resolver, and SUM function to work together. A unit test of SUM in isolation (e.g., sum([1,2,...,10])) would miss failures in range parsing, cell value resolution, or evaluation order. The real failure boundary is 'the full pipeline from formula to result', which requires multiple modules wired together.
behaviors.test_description: Create a cell engine. Set cells A1 through A10 to values 1 through 10. Create a formula =SUM(A1:A10) in cell B1. Verify that B1 evaluates to 55, and that the range resolver correctly identified and summed all 10 cells.

behaviors.description: A cell referencing another cell that contains a formula (e.g., =B1+1 where B1 is =A1*2) produces the correct cascaded result.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is 'dependent cell uses wrong value from upstream formula'. This requires the cell graph, dependency tracking, and evaluation order to be correct. A unit test of formula evaluation in isolation misses bugs where B1 doesn't recognize A1's computed value, uses stale values, or doesn't trigger re-evaluation. The test must verify that cascading through the cell graph works correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1=5, B1=A1*2, C1=B1+1. Verify that C1 evaluates to 11 (using A1's value, not hardcoded intermediate results). Change A1 to 3 and verify C1 re-evaluates to 7, confirming the cascaded dependency chain works.

behaviors.description: The engine detects a circular dependency (A1 references B1, B1 references A1) and returns a circular reference error instead of looping.
behaviors.justification: While cycle detection can be tested as an algorithm in isolation (unit test a graph traversal), the real failure is in the running engine: 'when the user creates a circular reference, does the engine detect it and return an error instead of hanging?' This requires cell references to be tracked in the actual cell graph and the cycle detector to be invoked during evaluation. A unit test of cycle-detection logic alone misses failures in how the engine integrates the detector.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1 with formula =B1 and B1 with formula =A1. Attempt to evaluate either cell. Verify the engine returns a circular-reference error (not an infinite loop, not silent failure). Verify error is propagated to both cells.

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP with an exact match returns the correct value from the lookup table, and returns an error when the key is not found.
behaviors.justification: In a real spreadsheet, VLOOKUP works with cell ranges (e.g., =VLOOKUP(key, A1:C10, 2)). The real failure could be 'VLOOKUP logic is broken' (catchable at Unit) or 'the range A1:C10 doesn't resolve' (requires Integration). To test the full behavior as described, the lookup table must come from cells, not a hardcoded array. This requires range resolution and cell reference integration.
behaviors.test_description: Create a lookup table in cells A1:C10 with keys in column A and values in columns B and C. Create a formula =VLOOKUP('key', A1:C10, 2) in another cell. Verify it returns the correct value for an existing key. Create another formula with a non-existent key and verify it returns an error (e.g., #N/A). Verify the range is correctly resolved from the cell addresses.

behaviors.description: The IF function correctly handles nested conditions (e.g., =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low")).
behaviors.justification: The example uses a cell reference (A1). To test this behavior as specified, the test must resolve the reference A1 and feed its value into the IF logic. A unit test of IF(cond, then, else) with hardcoded conditions misses bugs in cell reference resolution or incorrect value being compared. The real failure boundary is 'the cell engine correctly evaluates nested IFs that reference other cells'.
behaviors.test_description: Set A1=15. Create formula =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low"). Verify it returns "medium". Test edge cases: A1=5 (expect "low"), A1=25 (expect "high"). Verify that the cell reference A1 is resolved before evaluation, not baked into the formula.

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins cell values with the correct separator and handles empty cells by skipping them rather than inserting blank strings.
behaviors.justification: The behavior specifically mentions handling empty cells, which are accessed as cell references. To test that empty cells are skipped correctly, you need the cell engine to resolve cell references and pass actual values (or empty/null) to CONCATENATE. A unit test with hardcoded values and nulls might catch the skipping logic, but misses bugs in how empty cells are represented or resolved from the actual cell grid.
behaviors.test_description: Set A1="hello", B1="" (empty), C1="world". Create formula =CONCATENATE(A1, B1, C1, " "). Verify it returns "hello world" (B1 skipped, not "hello  world" with double space). Test with multiple consecutive empty cells to ensure robust skipping. Verify that cell references are resolved to their actual values (empty vs. zero vs. false) before concatenation.

behaviors.description: A formula referencing a cell in a different sheet (e.g., =Sheet2!A1+1) resolves correctly when the referenced sheet exists.
behaviors.justification: This behavior inherently requires multiple sheets (at least 2 modules). The real failure could be 'the parser doesn't recognize the sheet! syntax' (unit-testable) or 'the engine can't resolve the cross-sheet reference in the actual workbook' (requires integration). To test the full behavior, you must have an actual multi-sheet workbook structure and verify the reference resolves. This is a multi-module coordination problem.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with Sheet1 and Sheet2. In Sheet2, set cell A1=10. In Sheet1, create formula =Sheet2!A1+1. Verify it returns 11. Test that referencing a non-existent sheet (Sheet3) returns an error. Test that changing Sheet2.A1 causes the Sheet1 formula to re-evaluate. Verify the sheet reference is parsed and the correct sheet is accessed.

behaviors.description: When a referenced cell's value changes, all dependent formulas are recalculated in the correct topological order.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is 'the engine doesn't recalculate dependents in the right order, causing stale values'. A unit test of topological-sort logic alone (given a graph) misses bugs in how the engine triggers recalculation, applies the sort to real cells, or handles side effects. The test must verify that the full cell graph is traversed in the correct order and that dependent values reflect all upstream changes. This requires the dependency tracker and recalculation engine to be integrated.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1=1, B1=A1+1, C1=B1+1. Verify initial values: A1=1, B1=2, C1=3. Change A1 to 2. Verify recalculation happens in order: first A1=2, then B1=3, then C1=4. Use assertions or logging to confirm the order of recalculation. Test a more complex graph (diamond dependency, multiple branches) to verify topological correctness. Verify no cell uses stale values during recalculation.

behaviors.description: The undo operation after editing a formula restores both the formula text and all previously computed dependent values.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is 'undo restores formula text but not dependent values, or vice versa'. A unit test of undo-stack logic alone misses bugs in how the engine snapshots and restores cell state (both formula and computed values). To test the full behavior, you must create an actual state change, undo it, and verify both the formula and all dependent values are restored correctly. This requires the undo mechanism to be integrated with the cell engine and dependency system.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1=5, B1=A1*2, C1=B1+1. Verify C1=11. Edit A1's formula to =6 (changing it from a value). Verify B1 and C1 re-evaluate correctly. Undo the edit. Verify A1 is restored to 5, B1 is restored to 10, and C1 is restored to 11. Test multiple undo steps and verify the full state (all formulas and values) is restored, not just the edited cell.

behaviors.description: Saving a workbook to disk and loading it back produces identical cell values and formulas — no data loss or precision change in floating-point results.
behaviors.justification: The real failure involves actual disk I/O and file format handling. A unit test with mocked file I/O (in-memory buffers) might catch serialization-format bugs but would miss: floating-point precision loss during write/read, actual disk I/O failures, file corruption, or OS-level issues. The behavior explicitly requires testing the persistence mechanism end-to-end. This requires real filesystem interaction to catch data-loss or precision failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with a mix of formulas and computed values, including floating-point results from division and transcendental functions. Save to disk in the custom binary format. Close the workbook (or open a new instance without the in-memory state). Load the workbook from disk. Verify all cell formulas are identical to the original (byte-for-byte if applicable). Verify all computed values are numerically identical with no floating-point precision loss. Repeat with a large workbook to stress test serialization. Verify no data is lost and the workbook is fully functional after load.
